comments.author: ryan953
comments.body: We'll have to figure out why there are two names for things in different apis and consolidate. as a follow up

comments.author: ryan953
comments.body:
I think keeping the org-level and project-level as 1:1 as possible will be a benefit, even if it doesn't make sense. 

Let me contextualize that though with a train of thought:
- At the org-level validation will be hard, or at least annoying to resolve, as people typically push values up one field at a time. So it's possible that one field would need to be disabled in order for another to be set... that's annoying. 
- If we keep things 1:1 here then the complicated org-level stuff, and complicated-project level stuff are at least complicated together 🌈 . We can defer the problem of resolving conflicts at the same time that we do a migration into a better data structure and/or shape of the data
- On the other hand, if we _don't_ make things 1:1 here now, then we'll have to sort it all out again when we do a migration to another data structure and/or api. I want to make that migration happen so I'm ok with some cruft in the interim

comments.author: sentry-warden[bot]
comments.body:
**Organization option key mismatch causes setting to have no effect**

The new option registers with key `sentry:default_automated_run_stopping_point` but the code that consumes this value (in `seer/similarity/utils.py:587` and `tasks/seer/autofix.py:249`) reads from key `sentry:default_stopping_point`. Any value set via the API will be stored but never read, making the feature non-functional.

<details><summary>Verification</summary>

Traced the option key through all usages: the endpoint and serializer both use `sentry:default_automated_run_stopping_point` while `seer/similarity/utils.py:587` and `tasks/seer/autofix.py:249` read `sentry:default_stopping_point`. Confirmed via Grep that no code reads from `sentry:default_automated_run_stopping_point` except the serializer for display.

</details>

<details><summary>Also found at 1 additional location</summary>

- `src/sentry/seer/similarity/utils.py:587-588`

</details>

Identified by Warden `sentry-backend-bugs` · `3BT-XDW`
<!-- warden:v1:src/sentry/core/endpoints/organization_details.py:266:1c6f4b45 -->

comments.author: sentry-warden[bot]
comments.body:
**Pydantic validation error when coding_agent is 'seer' with integration_id**

When `coding_agent` is 'seer' (the default) and `coding_agent_integration_id` is not None, the code creates a `SeerAutomationHandoffConfiguration` with `target='seer'`. However, the Pydantic model's `target` field is `Literal['cursor_background_agent', 'claude_code_agent']`, which does not include 'seer'. This causes a `pydantic.ValidationError` that propagates up and could fail project creation. The API allows setting `defaultCodingAgentIntegrationId` without changing `defaultCodingAgent` from its default value.

<details><summary>Verification</summary>

Read `SeerAutomationHandoffConfiguration` in `seer_api_models.py` (line 86-90) which shows `target: Literal['cursor_background_agent', 'claude_code_agent']`. Verified `SEER_DEFAULT_CODING_AGENT_DEFAULT = 'seer'` in constants.py (line 732). Checked organization_details.py serializer and confirmed no cross-validation prevents setting integration_id when coding_agent is 'seer'. The condition on line 592 only checks truthiness and None, not whether the value is valid for the Literal type.

</details>

**Suggested fix:** Only create automation_handoff when coding_agent is a valid external agent (cursor_background_agent or claude_code_agent), not when it's 'seer'.

```suggestion
    # Only create automation_handoff for external agents (cursor_background_agent, claude_code_agent)
    # that require an integration_id. 'seer' is the internal agent and doesn't use handoff.
    if coding_agent in ("cursor_background_agent", "claude_code_agent") and (
        coding_agent_integration_id is not None
    ):
```

Identified by Warden `sentry-backend-bugs` · `5HH-HYB`
<!-- warden:v1:src/sentry/seer/similarity/utils.py:600:80256ef0 -->

comments.author: sentry-warden[bot]
comments.body:
**TypeError when saving defaultCodingAgent: str | None union type is not callable**

The `defaultCodingAgent` option tuple uses `str | None` as the type parameter. In `OrganizationSerializer.save()` at line 657, the code calls `type_(data[key])` to convert the value. However, `str | None` is a `types.UnionType` which cannot be called as a function, causing `TypeError: Cannot instantiate typing.Union` when saving a non-None value for this option. Other nullable options (e.g., `relayPiiConfig`, `apdexThreshold`) correctly use just `str` or `int` as the type parameter with `None` as the default.

<details><summary>Verification</summary>

Read the full organization_details.py file. Traced the ORG_OPTIONS tuple usage in OrganizationSerializer.save() (line 643-668). Confirmed that `type_` from the tuple is called as `type_(data[key])` at line 657. Compared with other nullable options (lines 127, 128, 177, 179) which use concrete types like `str`, `int`, `list` rather than union types.

</details>

**Suggested fix:** Use `str` as the type parameter instead of `str | None`. The None handling is already done at line 657 with the conditional `None if data[key] is None else type_(data[key])`.

```suggestion
    ("defaultCodingAgent", "sentry:seer_default_coding_agent", str, None),
```

<details><summary>Also found at 1 additional location</summary>

- `src/sentry/seer/similarity/utils.py:590-597`

</details>

Identified by Warden `sentry-backend-bugs` · `K7W-KRG`
<!-- warden:v1:src/sentry/core/endpoints/organization_details.py:244:320d7184 -->

comments.author: srest2021
comments.body:
Normalizing "seer" to None coding agent makes most sense to me, but happy to allow "seer" as well.

For context we don't allow "seer" or "seer_coding_agent" as a handoff target in [Sentry](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/ecb1c2553334f26c4dbb4d42ccb4e6f2d8c36cbd/src/sentry/seer/models/seer_api_models.py#L88). But we do allow it in [Seer](https://github.com/getsentry/seer/blob/e45b3f0829c7f1a7ce68427c80a61a9128e307a0/src/seer/automation/models.py#L1520).

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body:
### Uncaught Pydantic error on legacy coding agent values **Medium Severity** If an org has a legacy `sentry:seer_default_coding_agent` value (e.g., `"seer"` from the old default) stored alongside a non-null `integration_id`, the `SeerAutomationHandoffConfiguration` constructor will raise a Pydantic `ValidationError` because `target` only accepts `Literal["cursor_background_agent", "claude_code_agent"]`. This error is raised outside the `try/except` block, so it propagates uncaught and could crash project creation.
Additional Locations (1) - [`src/sentry/tasks/seer/autofix.py#L250-L259`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/22f98fe33e43ec7a00c4e50904ea2a8822705391/src/sentry/tasks/seer/autofix.py#L250-L259)

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body:
### Inconsistent read/write defaults for `defaultCodingAgent` **Medium Severity** The `ORG_OPTIONS` default for `defaultCodingAgent` was changed to `None`, but the serializer still uses `SEER_DEFAULT_CODING_AGENT_DEFAULT` (`"seer"`) as its read default. This means the API returns `"seer"` when the option is unset, yet the write-path audit-log comparison uses `None`. When a user saves without changing the coding agent, the validated value `"seer"` is compared against `None`, triggering a spurious audit log entry. The API docs example also shows `None`, contradicting the serializer. Either the `ORG_OPTIONS` default or the serializer default needs to be aligned.
Additional Locations (1) - [`src/sentry/api/serializers/models/organization.py#L737-L740`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/7a0759fbe908942d919f4fb68754e498a0cda491/src/sentry/api/serializers/models/organization.py#L737-L740)

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: ### Near-identical stopping point constants cause confusion **Low Severity** `SEER_DEFAULT_AUTOMATED_RUN_STOPPING_POINT_DEFAULT` (new, org-level) and `SEER_AUTOMATED_RUN_STOPPING_POINT_DEFAULT` (existing, project-level) are defined adjacently with the same value `"code_changes"` and nearly identical names. This makes it easy to accidentally use the wrong one. Reusing the existing constant or giving more clearly differentiated names would reduce this risk.

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: ### Test asserts wrong default value for coding agent **Medium Severity** `test_default_coding_agent_default` asserts `response.data["defaultCodingAgent"] is None`, but the serializer returns `SEER_DEFAULT_CODING_AGENT_DEFAULT` (`"seer"`) as the fallback when the org option is unset. `Organization.get_option("sentry:seer_default_coding_agent", SEER_DEFAULT_CODING_AGENT_DEFAULT)` returns `"seer"` when no option row exists. The previous assertion `== SEER_DEFAULT_CODING_AGENT_DEFAULT` was correct. This test will fail in CI.

comments.author: sentry[bot]
comments.body:
**Bug:** The migration task `configure_seer_for_existing_org` skips projects with existing stopping points, preventing the new `automation_handoff` configuration from being applied to them. Severity: HIGH
Suggested Fix Modify the logic to avoid skipping projects with existing stopping points entirely. Instead of the early `continue`, ensure the `default_handoff` configuration is appended to `preferences_to_set` for these projects, while preserving their existing `automated_run_stopping_point` value.
Prompt for AI Agent ``` Review the code at the location below. A potential bug has been identified by an AI agent. Verify if this is a real issue. If it is, propose a fix; if not, explain why it's not valid. Location: src/sentry/tasks/seer/autofix.py#L282-L283 Potential issue: The `configure_seer_for_existing_org` task is intended to migrate organization-level Seer defaults, including the new `automation_handoff` setting, to all existing projects. However, the logic at lines 282-283 contains a `continue` statement that skips any project that already has an `automated_run_stopping_point` set to `"open_pr"` or `"code_changes"`. This prevents the new `automation_handoff` configuration from being applied to these projects. As a result, when an organization enables an external coding agent like Cursor or Claude, existing projects with pre-configured stopping points will not receive the necessary handoff configuration, which breaks the external agent integration for them. ```
Did we get this right? :+1: / :-1: to inform future reviews.

comments.author: sentry[bot]
comments.body:
**Bug:** The code reads `sentry:seer_default_coding_agent` from the database without applying the new alias mapping, causing a `ValidationError` when passing un-aliased legacy values to Pydantic models. Severity: HIGH
Suggested Fix When reading the `sentry:seer_default_coding_agent` option from the database, apply the same aliasing logic that is used on the write path. This ensures that legacy values like `"cursor"` are transformed into the expected `"cursor_background_agent"` format before being passed to Pydantic models, preventing validation errors.
Prompt for AI Agent ``` Review the code at the location below. A potential bug has been identified by an AI agent. Verify if this is a real issue. If it is, propose a fix; if not, explain why it's not valid. Location: src/sentry/seer/similarity/utils.py#L588 Potential issue: The code reads the `sentry:seer_default_coding_agent` option from the database and passes it directly to Pydantic models in `set_default_project_auto_open_prs` and `configure_seer_for_existing_org`. However, the validation logic for this option was updated to alias values like `"cursor"` to `"cursor_background_agent"` only on write operations via the API. Legacy data in the database may contain un-aliased values. When these un-aliased values are read and passed to the Pydantic models, which expect the new aliased format, a `ValidationError` occurs. This error is silently caught, preventing Seer automation handoff from being configured for organizations with legacy data. ```

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: ### Migration overwrites valid "root_cause" and "solution" stopping points **Medium Severity** The stopping-point preservation check in `configure_seer_for_existing_org` only keeps `"open_pr"` and `"code_changes"`, but the new org-level `defaultAutomatedRunStoppingPoint` ChoiceField accepts all four values: `"root_cause"`, `"solution"`, `"code_changes"`, and `"open_pr"`. If a project already has `"root_cause"` or `"solution"` intentionally configured, the migration silently overwrites it with the org default. Any existing non-null stopping point that was deliberately set by a user deserves to be preserved.

comments.author: srest2021
comments.body:
Intended. See https://sentry.slack.com/archives/C0AAF0JD0GK/p1774895098789959?thread_ts=1774894912.268709&cid=C0AAF0JD0GK

> With the new pricing model based on the "Auto open PRs" toggle only "open_pr", "code_changes" can be stopping points.
"Auto open PRs" On -> Create PR
"Auto open PRs" Off -> code changes
So this condition checks if a project already has this stopping point. If it does we don't need to update it
> Now based on issue fixability we can stop early at RCA or solution sometimes (not go upto code changes) but that decision is not visible to the user.

comments.author: ryan953
comments.body:
is `allow_null=True,` needed?

```suggestion
    defaultAutomatedRunStoppingPoint = serializers.ChoiceField(
        choices=["code_changes", "open_pr"],
        required=False,
        allow_null=True,
    )
```

comments.body:
### Virtualized menu list silently swallows "no options" message **Medium Severity** `ScmVirtualizedMenuList` uses `Array.isArray(children) ? children : []` which discards non-array children. When a search yields no matching platforms, react-select passes a single `NoOptionsMessage` element (not an array) as `children` to the `MenuList`. This causes the "No options" message to be silently dropped, leaving users with a confusing empty dropdown and no feedback that their search had no results.
Additional Locations (1) - [`static/app/views/onboarding/scmPlatformFeatures.tsx#L436-L437`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/32ad7e68749404a8c7a74fb8e36508c74e9959ef/static/app/views/onboarding/scmPlatformFeatures.tsx#L436-L437)

comments.author: sentry[bot]
comments.body:
**Bug:** The `ScmVirtualizedMenuList` component doesn't accept or forward `innerRef` and `innerProps` from `react-select`, which is required for custom components. Severity: MEDIUM
Suggested Fix Update the `ScmVirtualizedMenuList` component to accept `innerRef` and `innerProps`. Assign `ref={innerRef}` to the scrollable `div` element and spread `{...innerProps}` onto it. This follows the standard pattern for custom `react-select` components.
Prompt for AI Agent ``` Review the code at the location below. A potential bug has been identified by an AI agent. Verify if this is a real issue. If it is, propose a fix; if not, explain why it's not valid. Location: static/app/views/onboarding/components/scmVirtualizedMenuList.tsx#L24-L28 Potential issue: The `ScmVirtualizedMenuList` component, used as a custom `MenuList` for `react-select`, does not accept and forward the `innerRef` and `innerProps` props. `react-select` relies on these props to manage the component's state, particularly for scrolling and accessibility. Without `innerRef`, `react-select` cannot control the scroll position during keyboard navigation. This results in a usability issue where, in long lists, navigating with arrow keys will not scroll the container to keep the focused option in view. The missing `innerProps` also prevents necessary event handlers and accessibility attributes from being applied. ```
Did we get this right? :+1: / :-1: to inform future reviews.

### Manual platform analytics missing for framework modal path **Medium Severity** When a user selects a base language (e.g. JavaScript, Python) in the manual picker, `handleManualPlatformSelect` opens the framework suggestion modal and returns early, so `trackAnalytics('onboarding.scm_platform_selected', ...)` is never called. The `applyPlatformSelection` callback used in the modal's `onConfigure`/`onSkip` handlers also lacks any analytics tracking. comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: ### VirtualizedMenuList drops react-select accessibility props **Medium Severity** `ScmVirtualizedMenuList` only destructures `children`, `maxHeight`, and `optionHeight`, silently discarding react-select's `innerRef` and `innerProps`. Without `innerRef` on the scroll container, react-select cannot programmatically scroll to the focused option during keyboard navigation, making arrow-key browsing effectively broken for items outside the visible area. The dropped `innerProps` also removes ARIA attributes (`role`, `id`) that screen readers depend on.

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body:
### Exit animations are dead code without AnimatePresence **Medium Severity** The `exit={{opacity: 0}}` prop on both `MotionStack` components has no effect because they are wrapped in `LayoutGroup`, not `AnimatePresence`. In framer-motion, exit animations only fire for direct children of `AnimatePresence` when they are removed from the tree. `LayoutGroup` only coordinates layout animations between siblings. As a result, when toggling between detected and manual picker views, the exiting view unmounts instantly instead of fading out — only the entering view fades in via `initial`/`animate`.
Additional Locations (1) - [`static/app/views/onboarding/scmPlatformFeatures.tsx#L429-L430`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/4f6966ff72ab99320f583e68e7ba53cb6593930f/static/app/views/onboarding/scmPlatformFeatures.tsx#L429-L430)

The project's `AGENTS.md` guideline explicitly states "Favor props over style attribute" for supported props — and the `Container` base (which `Flex`/layout components extend) supports `width`, `height`, and related props. The reviewer also flagged these same inline styles in the PR discussion.

Size shift is caused by changing the border-bottom width from 1px (selected style) to 3px (unselected style), using `box-sizing: border-box` will not prevent the overall height change here because the element is not of fixed size.

This is basically a crude style replication of the Button component's "chonky" style (which uses absolutely positioned psuedo elements to achieve this effect), given the styles here are likely to change it didn't seem worth devoting more time to an exact replication of that style.

comments.author: jaydgoss
comments.body:
Yeah we do need the Stack wrapper here, without it the two Texts become separate grid cells.

It needs to be Stack rather than Container, because Text renders as a span, so in a plain div they'd sit side by side instead of stacking vertically.

comments.author: jaydgoss
comments.body:
if we spread the container props onto Text, it becomes a direct grid child and displays as a block, which activates `text-box-trim: trim-both` and trims the line-height. The Container wrapper keeps the span inline so the full line-height is preserved.

Highlighted this [here](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/pull/111623#discussion_r3002493587) on another PR.

If `Stack` has a non-zero default gap (which the reviewer explicitly asked about — "does stack have a default gap?"), this introduces unwanted vertical spacing between the platform name and type text inside the card. The previous explicit `gap="0"` was intentional.

comments.author: sentry[bot]
comments.body:
**Bug:** Passing `e.detail` (which can be `None`) as `error_long` to the error function overrides the default error message, resulting in blank error pages for the GitHub integration. Severity: MEDIUM
Suggested Fix Only pass the `error_long` argument when `e.detail` is not `None`. This can be done by building a dictionary of keyword arguments conditionally or by using a conditional expression like `error_long=e.detail or ERR_INTEGRATION_INVALID_INSTALLATION_REQUEST`.
Prompt for AI Agent ``` Review the code at the location below. A potential bug has been identified by an AI agent. Verify if this is a real issue. If it is, propose a fix; if not, explain why it's not valid. Location: src/sentry/integrations/github/integration.py#L1330-L1334 Potential issue: In the refactored GitHub integration template flow, `GitHubPipelineError` exceptions are caught and `e.detail` is passed as the `error_long` argument to the error rendering function. However, several error cases like `GitHubInstallationError.MISSING_TOKEN` initialize `GitHubPipelineError` without a `detail`, making it `None`. This explicit `None` overrides the function's default `error_long` parameter. As a result, the Django template renders an empty string, and users see a blank error message instead of a descriptive one. ```
Did we get this right? :+1: / :-1: to inform future reviews.

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body:
### Missing error detail causes `None` in error templates **Medium Severity** `GitHubPipelineError` is raised without a `detail` for `PENDING_DELETION` and `USER_MISMATCH`, but the template-flow callers pass `e.detail` as `error_long` to the `error()` function. The old code explicitly passed `ERR_INTEGRATION_PENDING_DELETION` for pending deletion and used the function's default `ERR_INTEGRATION_INVALID_INSTALLATION_REQUEST` for user mismatch. Now `e.detail` is `None`, so the error template renders `None` instead of a meaningful message.
Additional Locations (1) - [`src/sentry/integrations/github/integration.py#L1078-L1079`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/21a3449dacc57adcfe7e7b73bfce1288ed44a52f/src/sentry/integrations/github/integration.py#L1078-L1079)

comments.author: sentry-warden[bot]
comments.body:
**Unvalidated installation_id allows hijacking arbitrary GitHub installations**

The `GithubOrganizationSelectionApiStep.handle_post` method accepts `installation_id` from POST data (line 1259) and binds it directly to pipeline state without validating that the authenticated user has access to that GitHub installation. When `validate_github_installation` is called (line 1275), it only validates ownership if an Integration record already exists in Sentry's database. For new installations, the untrusted `installation_id` passes through to `build_integration`, which uses the Sentry GitHub App's JWT (not the user's OAuth token) to fetch installation info, allowing any GitHub installation with the app installed to be connected to an attacker's Sentry org.

<details><summary>Verification</summary>

Traced the flow: (1) POST data `installation_id` is bound to state at line 1259-1260, (2) `validate_github_installation` at line 1275 checks lines 1067-1072 - returns `installation_id` without validation when no Integration exists, (3) `build_integration` lines 810-814 uses `get_installation_info` which authenticates via JWT not user's OAuth token. The `existing_installation_info` list (populated by user's OAuth in `exchange_github_oauth`) is never used to validate the `installation_id` parameter. The `chosen_installation_id` path has proper validation via `validate_org_installation_choice`, but the `installation_id` path does not.

</details>

**Suggested fix:** Validate that the `installation_id` is present in the user's `existing_installation_info` list before accepting it, similar to how `validate_org_installation_choice` validates `chosen_installation_id`.

```suggestion
                # Validate the installation_id is one the user has access to
                installation_info: list[GithubInstallationInfo] = (
                    pipeline.fetch_state("existing_installation_info") or []
                )
                valid_ids = [i["installation_id"] for i in installation_info]
                if installation_info and post_installation_id not in valid_ids:
                    lifecycle.record_failure(GitHubInstallationError.INVALID_INSTALLATION)
                    return PipelineStepResult.error(
                        GitHubInstallationError.INVALID_INSTALLATION
                    )
```

Identified by Warden `sentry-security` · `9XV-TXF`
<!-- warden:v1:src/sentry/integrations/github/integration.py:1260:e6e5ea63 -->

comments.author: sentry-warden[bot]
comments.body:
**KeyError when GitHub API response lacks 'installations' key**

Line 908 uses direct dict access `installed_orgs["installations"]` on the GitHub API response. If the API returns an error response, a malformed response, or an unexpected structure, this will raise `KeyError: 'installations'`. This matches the missing-key-access pattern from data-validation.md where external API responses are accessed without checking key existence.

<details><summary>Verification</summary>

Read client.py line 128-133 showing `get_user_info_installations` returns raw API response from `/user/installations`. Read integration.py line 901-908 confirming direct dict access without `.get()` or error handling. External API responses should never use direct key access per references/data-validation.md pattern A.

</details>

<details><summary>Also found at 1 additional location</summary>

- `src/sentry/integrations/github/integration.py:905-910`

</details>

Identified by Warden `sentry-backend-bugs` · `4X9-9HY`
<!-- warden:v1:src/sentry/integrations/github/integration.py:908:9b0a9a14 -->

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: Now I have all the information I need. Let me provide a clear answer about whether this issue existed in the old code. ## Analysis Summary **Yes, this problem existed in the old Django-driven views before the refactor.** The issue is NOT introduced by the refactor—it's a pre-existing vulnerability that was simply moved from the `GitHubIntegrationView` class method into the new standalone `_get_eligible_multi_org_installations` function. ### Evidence: Looking at the git diff, the old code had: ```python def _get_eligible_multi_org_installations( self, owner_orgs: list[str] ) -> list[GithubInstallationInfo]: installed_orgs = self.client.get_user_info_installations() return [ { "installation_id": str(installation.get("id")), "github_account": installation.get("account").get("login"), "avatar_url": installation.get("account").get("avatar_url"), } for installation in installed_orgs["installations"] # ⚠️ Direct dict access ... ] ``` The new code has the exact same pattern: ```python def _get_eligible_multi_org_installations( client: GithubSetupApiClient, owner_orgs: list[str] ) -> list[GithubInstallationInfo]: installed_orgs = client.get_user_info_installations() return [ { "installation_id": str(installation.get("id")), "github_account": installation.get("account").get("login"), "avatar_url": installation.get("account").get("avatar_url"), } for installation in installed_orgs["installations"] # ⚠️ Same direct dict access ... ] ``` The refactor simply extracted this method from a class into a standalone function without changing its logic. The KeyError vulnerability has always been there—if GitHub's API returns a malformed response or error without the `"installations"` key, both the old and new code would crash with a KeyError. ### Should we fix it? **Yes**, this should be fixed even though it's pre-existing. The fix is straightforward: use `.get("installations", [])` instead of `["installations"]` to safely handle missing keys. Would you like me to fix this issue along with the other Warden findings?

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: Perfect! Now I have all the information I need. Let me provide a comprehensive answer about whether this security vulnerability existed before the refactor. ## Analysis Summary **Yes, this security vulnerability existed in the old Django-driven views before the refactor.** However, there's a critical nuance that Warden correctly identified. ### Evidence from the Old Code: The old `GitHubInstallationApiStep.dispatch()` method (lines 1163-1169 in master): ```python installation_id = pipeline.fetch_state("installation_id") or request.GET.get( "installation_id", None ) if installation_id is None: return HttpResponseRedirect(self.get_app_url()) pipeline.bind_state("installation_id", installation_id) ``` **The old code had the same vulnerability**: it would accept `installation_id` from `request.GET` without validating that the authenticated user has access to that installation. ### The Validation Gap: Looking at lines 1188-1195 in the old code, the validation logic was: ```python try: integration = Integration.objects.get( external_id=installation_id, status=ObjectStatus.ACTIVE ) except Integration.DoesNotExist: return pipeline.next_step() # ⚠️ No validation for new installations ``` **Just like the new code**, if the Integration didn't exist in Sentry's database (meaning it's a new installation), the old code would proceed without validating that the user has access to that `installation_id`. ### The Attack Vector: Both the old and new code share the same vulnerability: 1. User completes OAuth and gets a list of installations they have access to in `existing_installation_info` 2. Attacker modifies the `installation_id` parameter to point to a *different* GitHub installation (one they don't have access to) 3. If that installation doesn't exist in Sentry's database yet, both old and new code accept it without checking if it's in the user's `existing_installation_info` list 4. The code then uses Sentry's GitHub App JWT (not the user's OAuth token) to fetch that installation's info and complete the setup ### Conclusion: **This is a pre-existing security vulnerability that was not introduced by the refactor.** Warden's suggestion to validate against `existing_installation_info` is correct and should be implemented to fix this vulnerability in the new code, but it's worth noting this has always been a security issue in the GitHub integration setup flow.

comments.author: evanpurkhiser
comments.body:
OK so I dug into this more and left a better comment here.. but actually what happens is that:

* When the user clicks "Install" on the integration on github side, it fires a webhook that creates the integration on our side, which includes details of *who* installed it.
* There's a race here, because clicking that button happens immediately before the user is brought back here where this lookup happens. But that's OK, we create the integration anyway later. 
  * Theoretically an attacker could try and hijack someones integration by munging the installation_id here, but it would have to happen between the window where the user installs the integration on githubs side, and we create an integration on our side with the installer IDs (or the webhook creates the integration). They would also need to know the installation ID that was used to install the integration milliseconds before.
* If there was an integration we validate that integration was installed by the same person who's doing the flow right now.

### API step missing sentinel "-1" installation handling **Medium Severity** `GithubOrganizationSelectionApiStep.get_step_data` returns the `"-1"` sentinel option (from `_build_installation_info_with_counts`) representing "Integrate with a new GitHub organization." However, `handle_post` passes `chosen_installation_id` directly to `validate_org_installation_choice` without checking for `"-1"` first. Since `"-1"` isn't in `existing_installation_info` pipeline state, validation fails with `MISSING_OWNER_PRIVILEGES`. comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body:
### API flow skips pending deletion check for chosen installations **Medium Severity** When `chosen_installation_id` is provided, `GithubOrganizationSelectionApiStep.handle_post` calls `validate_org_installation_choice` then immediately advances without calling `validate_github_installation`. This skips the pending-deletion check that the template flow performs. In the template flow, after choosing an installation, the pipeline proceeds to `GitHubInstallation` which calls `validate_github_installation` — checking for `OrganizationIntegration` records in `PENDING_DELETION` status. The API flow bypasses this entirely for the chosen-installation path.

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body:
### Stale node data never cleaned from nodeDataRef on unregister **Medium Severity** `unregisterNode` dispatches to the reducer to remove the node (and descendants) from `state.nodes`, but never deletes the corresponding entries from `nodeDataRef`. Since each mount of a `registerSeerContext`-wrapped component generates a fresh `uniqueId`, each mount/unmount cycle permanently leaks that ID's data entry in the `Map`. With `SeerContextProvider` at the app root (never unmounts), `nodeDataRef` grows unboundedly over the user session as components mount and unmount during navigation.
Additional Locations (1) - [`static/app/views/seerExplorer/contexts/seerContext.tsx#L124-L125`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/92c5ad0072d425ac16b02c56d04561a6410ff8c2/static/app/views/seerExplorer/contexts/seerContext.tsx#L124-L125)

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: ### Merge semantics in `updateNodeData` retains stale keys **Low Severity** `updateNodeData` shallow-merges incoming data with the existing entry (`{...existing, ...data}`). Since `useSeerContext(data)` passes the complete data object each render, any keys that are dropped between renders will persist as stale entries. A direct replacement (`nodeDataRef.current.set(nodeId, data)`) would match the expected semantics of the write overload.

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body:
### Dead `data` field on `SeerContextNode` is never read **Low Severity** The `data` field on `SeerContextNode` is always initialized to `{}` in the reducer's `REGISTER_NODE` case and is never read anywhere. All snapshot assembly in `buildTree` and `serializeState` reads data from the imperative `nodeDataRef` map (`nodeData.get(id)`), not from `node.data`. This field is vestigial from the old reducer-based data approach that the PR explicitly replaced with the `useRef` pattern. Keeping it adds confusion about where data actually lives.
Additional Locations (1) - [`static/app/views/seerExplorer/contexts/seerContextReducer.ts#L56-L57`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/7ad01dc7b8dfd53616c55e0e5c13d62b8004a12b/static/app/views/seerExplorer/contexts/seerContextReducer.ts#L56-L57)

comments.author: JonasBa
comments.body:
```suggestion
  context: Record<string, unknown> | undefined
```

Unless we expect anyone to call `useSeerContext` without any arguments, I think it's might be worth forcing folks to always pass something to this hook

On a slightly related note, but the current type forces callers to pass an object, which may not fit every use-case, and could force unnecessary writes down the line (like that useEffect on L218). Imagine someone who wants to might want to store a string or a an already memoed value, then the object type constraint would force them to go from

```
const computed = useMemo(() => ...,[])
useSeerContext({key: computed}) <- new obj reference 
```

this puts the callers at an awkward spot as a) they may need the computed value to be an array due to component logic requirements or b) they need to think of what key to identify the value with (this might be beneficial to the context itself)

If we instead allow for polymorphic arguments, the calls to our hook no longer require an object wrapper to satisfy the types, which imo makes them a tad bit more ergonomic, but I think it also at least gives us the opportunity to optimize the write path by using the dependency argument.

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: ### Non-serializable data silently dropped on first write **Medium Severity** `prevDataRef` is initialized to `''`, and when `JSON.stringify` throws (e.g. circular reference), `serialized` is set to `''`. Since `'' !== ''` is `false`, `updateNodeData` is never called — despite the comment on line 219 saying "always write." This means non-serializable data is silently dropped on the first write and on every subsequent write, because `prevDataRef.current` stays `''` and `serialized` keeps being `''`.

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body:
### Nullish coalescing silently converts explicit `null` data to `{}` **Low Severity** The write overload signature `useLLMContext(data: {} | null)` explicitly accepts `null`, and the write path correctly stores `null` in `nodeDataRef`. However, `buildTree` and `serializeState` both read data back using `nodeData.get(id) ?? {}`, and the `??` operator triggers on both `null` and `undefined`. This means explicitly-written `null` values are silently converted to `{}` in snapshots. The intent of `?? {}` is to default the "no data written" case (`undefined`), but it unintentionally also swallows `null`.
Additional Locations (1) - [`static/app/views/seerExplorer/contexts/llmContext.tsx#L100-L101`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/d658993404d0e6c53b580e9efa5f43ec0bb4c80a/static/app/views/seerExplorer/contexts/llmContext.tsx#L100-L101)

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body:
### Circular data breaks snapshot serialization **Medium Severity** `useLLMContext` catches `JSON.stringify` failures but still stores the original non-serializable `data` via `updateNodeData`. That allows circular values into snapshots from `getSnapshot`, which can fail later when the tree is serialized for transport, despite `llmContextTypes.ts` documenting a JSON-serializable snapshot contract.
Additional Locations (1) - [`static/app/views/seerExplorer/contexts/llmContextTypes.ts#L48-L53`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/fab82d04e3f565a9403387ccf07ef095eb52fa04/static/app/views/seerExplorer/contexts/llmContextTypes.ts#L48-L53)

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body:
### Providerless hook usage now hard-crashes **Low Severity** `useLLMContext` always calls `useLLMContextRegistry`, and the context is created with `strict: true`. Any use of `useLLMContext` or `registerLLMContext` outside `LLMContextProvider` throws immediately, so isolated renders (tests, stories, or non-app-shell mounts) crash instead of degrading gracefully.
Additional Locations (1) - [`static/app/views/seerExplorer/contexts/llmContext.tsx#L203-L204`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/fab82d04e3f565a9403387ccf07ef095eb52fa04/static/app/views/seerExplorer/contexts/llmContext.tsx#L203-L204)

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body:
### Snapshot version ignores data mutations **Medium Severity** `updateNodeData` mutates `nodeDataRef` without incrementing `state.version`, so `getSnapshot()` can return changed `nodes[*].data` with an unchanged `version`. Any consumer using `version` as a change token can miss context updates and treat new semantic state as stale.
Additional Locations (1) - [`static/app/views/seerExplorer/contexts/llmContextTypes.ts#L40-L42`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/fab82d04e3f565a9403387ccf07ef095eb52fa04/static/app/views/seerExplorer/contexts/llmContextTypes.ts#L40-L42)

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: ### Component-only read can leak whole tree **Medium Severity** `getLLMContext(true)` falls back to `ctx.getSnapshot()` when `nodeId` is missing, so the “component-only” path can return the full context tree. Calling `useLLMContext()` from a descendant that is not under `registerLLMContext` unexpectedly exposes unrelated branches instead of a scoped snapshot.

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: ### Unhandled exceptions in NotFound recovery code can crash handler **Medium Severity** The `get_project_seer_preferences` and `set_project_seer_preference` calls inside the `except NotFound` handler can raise `SeerApiError` or `SeerApiResponseValidationError`. In Python, exceptions raised within an `except` block are **not** caught by sibling `except` clauses of the same `try` statement, so the `except Exception` handler at the bottom won't catch these. This means a Seer API failure during the preference-clearing recovery path would propagate uncaught up through `_maybe_continue_pipeline` and `execute`, crashing the completion hook. Previously, the `NotFound` was caught by `except Exception`, which logged and returned cleanly—this change can regress that resilience.

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: ### Overly broad NotFound catch returns misleading error code **Low Severity** The `except NotFound` handler returns `error_code: "integration_not_found"` for every `NotFound` raised by `launch_coding_agents_for_run`, but that function raises `NotFound` for three distinct reasons: organization not found, integration not found, and autofix state not found. The Seer-side consumer of this error code will clear the `automation_handoff` preference whenever it sees `"integration_not_found"`, which means a transient "autofix state not found" error could incorrectly wipe a user's valid integration configuration.

Within the `except IntegrationNotFound` block, the code attempts to fetch a project using `Project.objects.get_from_cache(id=project_id)`. If `project_id` is `None`, this call raises an exception. This exception is then silently caught by a broad `except Exception` block, which prevents the intended logic—clearing the user's handoff preference from the Sentry database—from running. This silent failure undermines the fix's goal of cleaning up stale integration references, which can occur if the calling service sends a request without a `project_id`. ```
Did we get this right? :+1: / :-1: to inform future reviews.

The broad `except Exception` block silently swallows this error, meaning the Sentry DB preference cleanup — the core purpose of this fix — never executes. The function still returns the `error_code`, masking the fact that cleanup failed. Since this is an RPC endpoint with `project_id` newly added as optional, any caller that omits it will silently skip the stale preference cleanup.

comments.author: sentry[bot]
comments.body:
**Bug:** The circuit breaker incorrectly records HTTP error responses as successes because the status code checks are performed outside the `circuit_breaker_tracking` context manager. Severity: CRITICAL
Suggested Fix Move the logic that checks the `response.status_code` and raises `ApiHostError`, `ApiTimeoutError`, or `ClientError` inside the `with circuit_breaker_tracking(circuit_breaker):` block. This ensures that HTTP-level failures are caught by the context manager and correctly recorded as errors.
Prompt for AI Agent ``` Review the code at the location below. A potential bug has been identified by an AI agent. Verify if this is a real issue. If it is, propose a fix; if not, explain why it's not valid. Location: src/sentry/utils/sentry_apps/webhooks.py#L159-L168 Potential issue: The `circuit_breaker_tracking` context manager only wraps the `safe_urlopen` call, which does not raise exceptions for HTTP error status codes like 503 or 4xx. Instead, it returns a response object. The code that checks for these error status codes and raises corresponding exceptions is located outside of the `with` block. Consequently, when a webhook returns an HTTP error, the context manager exits normally and calls `breaker.record_success()`. This causes the circuit breaker to under-report the error rate, preventing it from tripping for consistently failing webhook endpoints and defeating its purpose. ```
Did we get this right? :+1: / :-1: to inform future reviews.

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body:
### Circuit breaker records success for HTTP error responses **Medium Severity** The `circuit_breaker_tracking` context manager wraps only the `safe_urlopen` call, so its `else` branch calls `record_success()` as soon as `safe_urlopen` returns any HTTP response. Response status code validation (503, 504, 4xx, other 5xx) happens *after* the context manager exits. This means HTTP error responses are recorded as successes in the circuit breaker, inflating `total_requests` in the `RateBasedTripStrategy` without incrementing `error_count`. The error-rate formula `error_count / total_requests` is diluted, making the breaker significantly harder to trip for apps consistently returning error status codes.
Additional Locations (1) - [`src/sentry/utils/sentry_apps/webhooks.py#L254-L278`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/ab52d21548506001f066121175204206fbcaee5d/src/sentry/utils/sentry_apps/webhooks.py#L254-L278)

comments.author: sentry[bot]
comments.body:
**Bug:** The circuit breaker for Sentry App webhooks uses a per-app slug for its metrics key, creating high-cardinality data because a `metrics_key` override is not provided. Severity: HIGH
Suggested Fix Add a `metrics_key` to the `sentry-app.webhook` circuit breaker configuration in `src/sentry/options/defaults.py`. Set its value to a static string, such as `"sentry-app.webhook"`, to ensure metrics are aggregated at a system level instead of per-app.
Prompt for AI Agent ``` Review the code at the location below. A potential bug has been identified by an AI agent. Verify if this is a real issue. If it is, propose a fix; if not, explain why it's not valid. Location: src/sentry/utils/sentry_apps/webhooks.py#L91-L95 Potential issue: The `CircuitBreaker` in `sentry_apps/webhooks.py` is initialized with a `key` that is unique to each Sentry App slug (`sentry-app.webhook.{sentry_app.slug}`). The corresponding configuration in `defaults.py` does not specify a `metrics_key`. As a result, the `metrics_key` defaults to the high-cardinality `key`, causing metrics to be emitted with per-app granularity. This will create a unique metric series for every Sentry App, which can exceed monitoring service cardinality limits and significantly increase billing costs once the feature is enabled. ```

comments.author: sentry-warden[bot]
comments.body:
**ValueError when external webhook returns non-numeric Sentry-Hook-Project header**

The `int(p)` conversion on line 251 will raise `ValueError` if the external Sentry App webhook returns a non-numeric string in the `Sentry-Hook-Project` response header. Since this header comes from an external service (the Sentry App's webhook endpoint), it is untrusted input that could contain any string value like "abc", "project_1", or "123.45". An unhandled `ValueError` would cause the webhook processing to fail.

<details><summary>Verification</summary>

Read webhooks.py to confirm the header comes from `response.headers.get()` which returns external data. Verified `add_request` expects `project_id: int | None`. Checked grep results showing this is the only place doing int conversion - external_requests/utils.py passes the raw string. The conversion lacks try/except ValueError handling.

</details>

**Suggested fix:** Wrap the int conversion in a try/except to handle non-numeric header values gracefully

```suggestion
        project_id: int | None = None
        if p := response.headers.get("Sentry-Hook-Project"):
            try:
                project_id = int(p)
            except ValueError:
                pass
```

Identified by Warden `sentry-backend-bugs` · `UVG-8SS`
<!-- warden:v1:src/sentry/utils/sentry_apps/webhooks.py:251:f6a13e4c -->

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body:
### Unhandled breaker exceptions can swallow webhook results **Medium Severity** The `circuit_breaker_tracking` context manager calls `breaker.record_error()` and `breaker.record_success()` without any try/except protection. Both methods make unprotected Redis calls (via `limiter.use_quotas`). On the success path, if `record_success()` raises a Redis exception, a successfully-sent webhook's response is lost — the buffer never logs it, callers see a Redis error, and the webhook may be retried (causing duplicates). On the error path, if `record_error()` raises, the original exception (e.g., `Timeout`) is replaced by the Redis error, altering retry behavior upstream.
Additional Locations (1) - [`src/sentry/utils/sentry_apps/webhooks.py#L208-L210`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/0c029372556e472ea58ab9fe9cf5b6a3f17bc59c/src/sentry/utils/sentry_apps/webhooks.py#L208-L210)

comments.author: sentry-warden[bot]
comments.body:
**Type change breaks project lookup - errorUrl will never be generated**

Changing `project_id` from `int` to `str` breaks downstream code in `request.py`. The serializer collects project_ids and queries `Project.objects.filter(id__in=project_ids)` (line 24), then builds a dict keyed by integer `project.id`. The subsequent lookup `projects_by_id.get(item.data.get("project_id"))` (line 34) now passes a string to a dict with int keys, always returning None. This causes the `errorUrl` field to never be included in webhook request responses.

<details><summary>Verification</summary>

Read request_buffer.py lines 143-195 showing the type change and storage of project_id. Read request.py lines 20-37 showing project_ids collected into a set, passed to Project.objects.filter(id__in=), then looked up via projects_by_id.get(item.data.get("project_id")). With string project_id stored in Redis, the int-keyed dict lookup fails. Also verified webhooks.py line 257 passes response.headers.get() which returns str.

</details>

**Suggested fix:** Convert the project_id header value to int before storing, keeping the parameter type as int. The HTTP header value is a string, but the database lookup requires an integer.

```suggestion
        project_id: int | None = None,
```

Identified by Warden `sentry-backend-bugs` · `MAL-A93`
<!-- warden:v1:src/sentry/utils/sentry_apps/request_buffer.py:150:875a8877 -->

*Resolved in [`c681859`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/commit/c6818590977df7f6d2867b8665c6b3a9d61ddbdf)* **Bug:** The `circuit_breaker_tracking` context manager only catches `WebhookTimeoutError`, ignoring other exceptions like `requests.Timeout`. This can render the circuit breaker ineffective or cause it to under-report errors. Severity: MEDIUM
Suggested Fix Modify the `except` block within the `circuit_breaker_tracking` context manager to catch a broader range of exceptions, such as `Exception`. This would align the implementation with the PR description's intent to record all failures and ensure the circuit breaker accurately tracks the true error rate from all sources, not just the custom `WebhookTimeoutError`.
Prompt for AI Agent ``` Review the code at the location below. A potential bug has been identified by an AI agent. Verify if this is a real issue. If it is, propose a fix; if not, explain why it's not valid. Location: src/sentry/utils/sentry_apps/circuit_breaker.py#L26-L34 Potential issue: The `circuit_breaker_tracking` context manager only catches `WebhookTimeoutError`. It does not catch other common network exceptions like `requests.exceptions.Timeout` or `ConnectionError`. This means that if the `organizations:sentry-app-webhook-hard-timeout` feature flag is disabled, the circuit breaker will not record any timeout errors at all, as `WebhookTimeoutError` will never be raised. Furthermore, other network failures will not be recorded, causing the circuit breaker's error rate calculation to be understated. This makes the feature less effective, as it will not trip when it should for a significant class of failures. ```

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body:
### Test expects `Timeout` to trigger `record_error`, but implementation only catches `WebhookTimeoutError` **High Severity** The `circuit_breaker_tracking` context manager only catches `WebhookTimeoutError` (line 28 of `circuit_breaker.py`), but `test_timeout_calls_record_error` sets `mock_safe_urlopen.side_effect = Timeout()` (from `requests.exceptions`) and asserts `record_error` was called. These are entirely separate exception hierarchies — `WebhookTimeoutError(Exception)` vs `Timeout(RequestException(IOError))` — so `Timeout` will propagate without triggering the `except WebhookTimeoutError` handler, and `record_error` is never called. The test assertion will fail. Either the context manager needs to also catch `Timeout`/`ConnectionError`, or the test needs to use `WebhookTimeoutError`.
Additional Locations (1) - [`src/sentry/utils/sentry_apps/circuit_breaker.py#L27-L34`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/26eee2c5995d4dbcd1d718201aa03c61b5ee730f/src/sentry/utils/sentry_apps/circuit_breaker.py#L27-L34)

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: ### Docstring placed after import won't be recognized **Low Severity** The docstring for `circuit_breaker_tracking` is placed after the `from ... import WebhookTimeoutError` statement on line 14. Python only treats a string literal as a function's `__doc__` if it's the very first expression in the body. Since the import is first, this string becomes a no-op expression and the function will have no discoverable documentation.

### Circuit breaker errors can crash webhook delivery for published apps **Medium Severity** `_create_circuit_breaker` and `_circuit_breaker_allows_request` (which calls `should_allow_request()`) are invoked inside a `try` block that only catches specific exception types (`WebhookTimeoutError`, `Timeout`, `ConnectionError`, etc.). An unexpected exception from these — such as a Redis failure inside `should_allow_request()` → `_should_trip()` → `check_within_quotas()`, or a `KeyError` from a malformed options config — would propagate uncaught, causing the webhook to fail for published apps. This is inconsistent with `circuit_breaker_tracking`, which carefully wraps `record_error()` and `record_success()` in `try/except Exception` to prevent circuit breaker infrastructure issues from breaking webhook delivery.
Additional Locations (1) - [`src/sentry/utils/sentry_apps/circuit_breaker.py#L28-L39`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/3688925c42f39b072bd5e7dd1430ccdc50694ef7/src/sentry/utils/sentry_apps/circuit_breaker.py#L28-L39)

comments.body: ### Untracked exceptions skew circuit breaker error rate calculation **Low Severity** The `circuit_breaker_tracking` context manager only calls `record_error` for `WebhookTimeoutError` and `record_success` on normal exit, but exceptions like `Timeout`, `ConnectionError`, and `ChunkedEncodingError` fall through without calling either method. Since `RateBasedTripStrategy` computes error rate as `error_count / total_requests_counted`, and `total_requests_counted` only includes calls to `record_error` + `record_success`, these untracked exceptions reduce the denominator and inflate the observed error rate. Cursor Bugbot has reviewed your changes and found 2 potential issues.
Autofix Details [Bugbot Autofix](https://cursor.com/docs/bugbot#autofix) prepared fixes for both issues found in the latest run. - ✅ Fixed: **Extracted `is_provider_enabled` drops `actor` parameter from feature check** - Added optional actor parameter to is_provider_enabled() and updated organization_config_integrations endpoint to pass request.user as actor, restoring user-level feature flag targeting capability. - ✅ Fixed: **Unused `Channel` namedtuple copied into staging integration** - Removed unused Channel namedtuple and its import from slack staging integration file.

Or push these changes by commenting: ``` @cursor push df894c901e ```
Preview (getsentry/sentry@df894c901e) ```diff diff --git a/src/sentry/integrations/api/endpoints/organization_config_integrations.py b/src/sentry/integrations/api/endpoints/organization_config_integrations.py --- a/src/sentry/integrations/api/endpoints/organization_config_integrations.py +++ b/src/sentry/integrations/api/endpoints/organization_config_integrations.py @@ -59,7 +59,9 @@ if provider_key: providers = [p for p in providers if p.key == provider_key] - providers = list(filter(lambda p: is_provider_enabled(p, organization), providers)) + providers = list( + filter(lambda p: is_provider_enabled(p, organization, actor=request.user), providers) + ) providers.sort(key=lambda i: i.key) diff --git a/src/sentry/integrations/base.py b/src/sentry/integrations/base.py --- a/src/sentry/integrations/base.py +++ b/src/sentry/integrations/base.py @@ -587,7 +587,9 @@ def is_provider_enabled( - provider: IntegrationProvider, organization: Organization | RpcOrganization + provider: IntegrationProvider, + organization: Organization | RpcOrganization, + actor: Any | None = None, ) -> bool: from sentry import features @@ -595,4 +597,4 @@ return True provider_key = provider.key.replace("_", "-") feature_flag_name = "organizations:integrations-%s" % provider_key - return features.has(feature_flag_name, organization) + return features.has(feature_flag_name, organization, actor=actor) diff --git a/src/sentry/integrations/slack/staging/integration.py b/src/sentry/integrations/slack/staging/integration.py --- a/src/sentry/integrations/slack/staging/integration.py +++ b/src/sentry/integrations/slack/staging/integration.py @@ -1,7 +1,6 @@ from __future__ import annotations import logging -from collections import namedtuple from collections.abc import Mapping from typing import Any @@ -18,9 +17,7 @@ _logger = logging.getLogger("sentry.integrations.slack") -Channel = namedtuple("Channel", ["name", "id"]) - class SlackStagingIntegrationProvider(SlackIntegrationProvider): key = IntegrationProviderSlug.SLACK_STAGING.value name = "Slack (Staging)" ```

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body:
### Extracted `is_provider_enabled` drops `actor` parameter from feature check **Medium Severity** The newly extracted `is_provider_enabled` function calls `features.has(feature_flag_name, organization)` without the `actor` parameter. The original inline version in `organization_config_integrations.py` passed `actor=request.user`. This means any feature flag evaluation that depends on user-level targeting (e.g., staff-only rollouts) will no longer work correctly for feature-flagged integration providers. The function signature doesn't accept an `actor` argument, so callers like `initialize_integration_pipeline` in `pipeline.py` also can't pass one.
Additional Locations (1) - [`src/sentry/integrations/api/endpoints/organization_config_integrations.py#L61-L62`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/678d08b0d069e7ab26f66d558e7c3a53e3cc8dd0/src/sentry/integrations/api/endpoints/organization_config_integrations.py#L61-L62)

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: ### Unused `Channel` namedtuple copied into staging integration **Low Severity** The `Channel` namedtuple is defined in `staging/integration.py` but never used anywhere in the file. It was copied from the parent `slack/integration.py` module unnecessarily.

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: ### Stale feature flag reference in frontend code **Medium Severity** The removal of the `organizations:slack-staging-app` feature flag from `temporary.py` leaves a stale reference in `static/app/views/settings/organizationIntegrations/integrationDetailedView.tsx` at line 333, where `organization.features.includes('slack-staging-app')` is checked to show a staging install button. Since the flag definition is removed, this condition will now always be `false`, silently breaking any existing staging button functionality that relied on it. If the old staging button is intentionally deprecated, the frontend code referencing it needs cleanup.

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: ### Hardcoded "slack" string in shared helper message **Low Severity** The `_send_slack_notification` helper is shared between both `send_slack` and `send_slack_staging` commands, but the channel-name error message on line 116 still hardcodes `"slack channel name"` instead of using the `provider_label` parameter. When invoked for `slack-staging`, the message will incorrectly say "slack channel name" instead of "Slack (Staging) channel name."

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: ### Staging spec inherits production `send_incident_alert_notification` without override **Medium Severity** `SlackStagingMessagingSpec` inherits `send_incident_alert_notification` from `SlackMessagingSpec` without overriding it. The parent implementation directly calls the hardcoded `send_incident_alert_notification` utility function from `sentry.integrations.slack.utils.notifications`, which works based on `integration_id` and doesn't distinguish between slack and slack-staging. While this may function by coincidence (the integration object carries the correct token), the `identity_view_set` property is also not overridden, meaning staging uses the same link/unlink identity views as production Slack, which could cause identity-linking flows to use the wrong provider context.

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: ### Staging parser missing dedicated `webhook_identifier` for outbox routing **Medium Severity** `SlackStagingRequestParser` inherits `webhook_identifier = WebhookProviderIdentifier.SLACK` from `SlackRequestParser` without overriding it. There is no `WebhookProviderIdentifier.SLACK_STAGING` entry in `WebhookProviderIdentifier`. This means hybrid-cloud outbox/webhook payloads from the staging integration are identified as regular Slack webhooks, potentially causing routing or deduplication issues in multi-silo deployments.

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: ### Unlinked alert only checks `slack`, not `slack_staging` **Low Severity** `unlinkedSlackOrgs` is hardcoded to `getUnlinkedOrgs('slack')` and never checks `'slack_staging'`. Users with a `slack_staging` integration who haven't linked their identity won't see the `UnlinkedAlert` banner prompting them to link, even though the staging provider is now a supported delivery method.

comments.author: sentry[bot]
comments.body:
*Resolved in [`3867ced`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/commit/3867ced4cf6c2365838d364410c5333c5ba76ebc)* **Bug:** The `metrics.incr()` call in `_send_slack_notification` hardcodes the instance prefix to `"slack."` causing staging provider metrics to be incorrectly merged with production metrics. Severity: MEDIUM
Suggested Fix Modify the `metrics.incr()` call in `_send_slack_notification` to dynamically use the provider's name in the `instance` parameter. For example: `instance=f"{provider.name.lower()}.{notification.metrics_key}.notification"`. This will correctly separate metrics for `slack` and `slack_staging`.
Prompt for AI Agent ``` Review the code at the location below. A potential bug has been identified by an AI agent. Verify if this is a real issue. If it is, propose a fix; if not, explain why it's not valid. Location: src/sentry/integrations/slack/notifications.py#L59-L63 Potential issue: The `_send_slack_notification` function uses a hardcoded `"slack."` prefix for the `instance` parameter in its `metrics.incr()` call. This happens regardless of whether the notification is for the production or staging Slack provider. As a result, metrics for Slack Staging notifications will be incorrectly attributed to the production Slack namespace, preventing separate monitoring and debugging of the new staging provider feature. The `provider` parameter passed to the function is ignored for metrics recording. ```
Did we get this right? :+1: / :-1: to inform future reviews.

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body:
### Spurious UnlinkedAlert shown for slack_staging to all slack users **Medium Severity** When a user has `slack` selected as their delivery method but no `slack_staging` integration installed, `getUnlinkedOrgs('slack_staging')` returns ALL organizations (since none have a slack_staging identity linked). The outer condition only checks if ANY provider in `ALLOWED_PROVIDERS` is selected, so it triggers when just `slack` is selected. This causes a second, confusing `UnlinkedAlert` to appear listing every organization — the alert text doesn't distinguish which Slack variant it refers to. Practically every user with Slack notifications will see this spurious warning.
Additional Locations (1) - [`static/app/views/settings/account/notifications/notificationSettingsByType.tsx#L359-L360`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/3867ced4cf6c2365838d364410c5333c5ba76ebc/static/app/views/settings/account/notifications/notificationSettingsByType.tsx#L359-L360)

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: ### Staging URLs missing identity linking view URL patterns **Low Severity** The production `slack/urls.py` appends identity view URL patterns via `SlackMessagingSpec().get_identity_view_set_url_patterns()`, but the staging `urls.py` does not include the equivalent `SlackStagingMessagingSpec().get_identity_view_set_url_patterns()` call. This means `link-identity`, `unlink-identity`, `link-team`, and `unlink-team` views are not registered under the `/extensions/slack-staging/` prefix. While the existing `build_linking_url` functions hardcode production URL names, this omission could cause issues if staging-specific identity link URLs are expected.

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body:
### Staging delivery method exposed to all users unconditionally **Medium Severity** The `slack_staging` delivery method is unconditionally added to `SUPPORTED_PROVIDERS` and to the `provider` field choices in `fields.tsx`. This means **all** users will see "Slack (Staging)" as a notification delivery method option in their account settings — not just users in organizations with the `integrations-slack-staging` feature flag. Selecting it without the staging integration installed would silently drop notifications. The integration installation is correctly feature-flag-gated on the backend, but the notification settings UI is not.
Additional Locations (1) - [`static/app/views/settings/account/notifications/constants.tsx#L1-L6`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/4ac07d3be0cb0bdf3bb991d1974d152dc6999134/static/app/views/settings/account/notifications/constants.tsx#L1-L6)

comments.author: sentry-warden[bot]
comments.body:
**QuerySubscription.DoesNotExist not handled in get_metric_issue_context**

The `get_metric_issue_context` call at line 33 invokes `MetricIssueContext.from_group_event()` which calls `QuerySubscription.objects.get(id=...)` without handling `DoesNotExist`. If the subscription is deleted between alert creation and notification rendering, this will crash the entire render method. This matches pattern Check 2 (Missing Record / Stale Reference) with 81 issues and 1.4M events in production.

<details><summary>Verification</summary>

Read /src/sentry/incidents/typings/metric_detector.py line 232-234 confirming `_get_subscription` uses bare `.get()` without try/except. Traced call path from render() -> get_metric_issue_context() -> from_group_event() -> _get_subscription(). No exception handling exists in any layer for DoesNotExist.

</details>

**Suggested fix:** Wrap the get_metric_issue_context call in a try/except block to handle QuerySubscription.DoesNotExist gracefully by capturing the exception and returning a minimal fallback.

```suggestion
from django.core.exceptions import ObjectDoesNotExist
        try:
            metric_issue_context = MetricAlertNotificationData.get_metric_issue_context(event)
        except ObjectDoesNotExist as e:
            sentry_sdk.capture_exception(e)
            return SlackRenderable(
                blocks=[],
                text="Unable to render metric alert notification - referenced data no longer exists",
            )
```

<details><summary>Also found at 1 additional location</summary>

- `src/sentry/notifications/platform/templates/metric_alert.py:143-143`

</details>

Identified by Warden `sentry-backend-bugs` · `PH7-3LZ`
<!-- warden:v1:src/sentry/notifications/platform/slack/renderers/metric_alert.py:33:0f270ef7 -->

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: ### Test assertions don't match round-tripped input values **Medium Severity** The `test_round_trip_none_threshold_type` test creates an `AlertContext` with `resolve_threshold=None`, `alert_threshold=None`, and `detection_type=AlertRuleDetectionType.STATIC`, but after round-tripping through `SerializableAlertContext`, asserts the values are `2.5`, `7.5`, and `AlertRuleDetectionType.PERCENT` respectively. The `from_alert_context` and `to_alert_context` methods faithfully preserve these values, so the round-tripped result will still have `None`, `None`, and `STATIC`. These three assertions will fail.

comments.author: Christinarlong
comments.body: true but original doesn't do any handling soooo :hides:

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body:
### Duplicate uncached Detector queries in render path **Medium Severity** The `detector` property performs an uncached `Detector.objects.get()` on every access. In the renderer, both `data.serialized_alert_rule` and `data.serialized_detector` internally call `self.detector`, resulting in two redundant DB queries for the same `Detector` object. Because the model is frozen Pydantic, `@cached_property` won't work. Each of those serializer properties also triggers a full `serialize()` call. This doubles the DB cost of fetching + serializing the detector on every metric alert notification when chartcuterie is enabled.
Additional Locations (1) - [`src/sentry/notifications/platform/slack/renderers/metric_alert.py#L41-L49`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/0e498951f63482a1bee7f537d456da0e0c9b1a59/src/sentry/notifications/platform/slack/renderers/metric_alert.py#L41-L49)

comments.author: Christinarlong
comments.body: ugh yes sadly we can't really do @cached_property on a pydantic model

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: ### Metric alert color information silently dropped during rendering **Medium Severity** `SlackIncidentsMessageBuilder.build()` returns a dict containing `blocks`, `text`, and `color` keys. The `color` field encodes alert severity (red for critical, yellow for warning, green for resolved). The existing metric alert path in `slack/utils/notifications.py` preserves the color by wrapping blocks inside a Slack `attachments` payload: `{"blocks": ..., "color": ...}`. Here, only `blocks` and `text` are extracted into the `SlackRenderable`, and `send_notification` sends them as top-level `blocks`/`text` to `chat_postMessage`, which doesn't support a colored sidebar. This means metric alert notifications sent through the notification platform will lose the severity color indicator that the existing path provides.

comments.body: The eventstore querying piece specifically. The instance checks and type validations are fine. I think we want to minimize the queries and lookups we do in these data objects since the end goal is for them to be fully self-contained serializable objects with all the primitives required to render a template.

comments.author: GabeVillalobos
comments.body: Normally I'd agree that touching the underlying layer is tricky, but in this case (iirc the history behind this), it's meant to be a DTO-esque object for carrying state across action -> notification code already.

comments.author: GabeVillalobos
comments.body: Yeah agreed, I don't think we'd want to retry on these either. It's more of a question of: should we handle this gracefully and maybe log/create a sentry issue, or should we let this raise and hit the taskbroker handling/let the caller handle this instead? Maybe it'd be good to let the service handle and rewrap these as a new `RenderError` type that rewraps this as something more meaningful. wdyt?

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: ### Color always included as empty string, violating NotRequired **Medium Severity** The `SlackRenderable` declares `color` as `NotRequired[str]`, meaning it can be omitted. However, `slack_body.get("color", "")` always sets `color` to at least an empty string — even when `SlackIncidentsMessageBuilder` intentionally omits `color` (e.g., when status doesn't map to a known level, `_build_blocks` skips it). An empty string is not a valid Slack attachment color value ("good", "warning", "danger", or hex). When the `send` path is updated to forward `color` to the Slack API, this will send invalid color values instead of omitting the field.

comments.author: sentry[bot]
comments.body:
**Bug:** The `if/elif` block checking the `data` type is not exhaustive. A new subclass of `BaseMetricAlertNotificationData` would cause an `UnboundLocalError` because `metric_issue_context` would not be assigned. Severity: MEDIUM
Suggested Fix Add a final `else` block to the `if/elif` chain to handle unexpected types. This block should raise a `ValueError` to fail fast and make it clear that a new data type is not supported, preventing an `UnboundLocalError` downstream.
Prompt for AI Agent ``` Review the code at the location below. A potential bug has been identified by an AI agent. Verify if this is a real issue. If it is, propose a fix; if not, explain why it's not valid. Location: src/sentry/notifications/platform/slack/renderers/metric_alert.py#L69-L72 Potential issue: The `render` method uses an `if/elif` structure to handle two subclasses of `BaseMetricAlertNotificationData`: `MetricAlertNotificationData` and `ActivityMetricAlertNotificationData`. However, it lacks a final `else` case. If a new subclass of `BaseMetricAlertNotificationData` is introduced in the future and passed to this renderer, neither condition will match. This will prevent the `metric_issue_context` variable from being initialized, leading to an `UnboundLocalError` when it is accessed later in the function. This is a latent bug that will cause a runtime crash when the data model is extended. ```
Did we get this right? :+1: / :-1: to inform future reviews.

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body:
### Mock patches wrong module path for eventstore **High Severity** The `@patch` decorator targets `sentry.notifications.platform.templates.metric_alert.eventstore.backend.get_event_by_id`, but `eventstore` is imported in `sentry.notifications.platform.slack.renderers.metric_alert`, not in `templates.metric_alert`. Since `templates/metric_alert.py` has no `eventstore` attribute, `patch` will raise `AttributeError` at test setup, causing all three affected tests to fail. The correct path is `sentry.notifications.platform.slack.renderers.metric_alert.eventstore.backend.get_event_by_id`.
Additional Locations (2) - [`tests/sentry/notifications/platform/slack/renderers/test_metric_alert.py#L143-L145`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/0eb7c28d0292b319e9e3155747b53a2941286c00/tests/sentry/notifications/platform/slack/renderers/test_metric_alert.py#L143-L145) - [`tests/sentry/notifications/platform/slack/renderers/test_metric_alert.py#L172-L174`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/0eb7c28d0292b319e9e3155747b53a2941286c00/tests/sentry/notifications/platform/slack/renderers/test_metric_alert.py#L172-L174)

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: ### Unbound variable when data is base class **Low Severity** `metric_issue_context` is only assigned inside the `if isinstance(data, MetricAlertNotificationData)` / `elif isinstance(data, ActivityMetricAlertNotificationData)` branches. If `data` passes the `BaseMetricAlertNotificationData` check but matches neither subclass (e.g., a future third subclass), `metric_issue_context` will be unbound when used later, causing an `UnboundLocalError`. An `else` branch raising an error would make this explicit.

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body: ### Test expects `model_config` in pydantic v1 dict output **Medium Severity** The assertion expects `"model_config": {"frozen": True}` to appear in `open_period_context.dict()` output. The project uses pydantic v1.10.26 (confirmed in `uv.lock`), where `model_config = ConfigDict(frozen=True)` is an unannotated class attribute — not a model field — so `.dict()` will not include it. This test will fail.

comments.author: Christinarlong
comments.body: bro this was literally failing without this 😭

comments.author: cursor[bot]
comments.body:
### Pydantic v1 `model_config` leaks into serialized output **Medium Severity** The project pins `pydantic>=1.10.26,<2`, which uses `class Config:` for model configuration. Using `model_config = ConfigDict(frozen=True)` (Pydantic v2 style) on `OpenPeriodContext` and `SerializableAlertContext` means the models likely aren't actually frozen, and `model_config` leaks into `.dict()` output as a spurious data field. The test assertion at line 266 confirms this by including `"model_config": {"frozen": True}` in the expected dict. Other models in this codebase (e.g., `NotificationData`, `NotificationTarget`) correctly use `class Config:`.
Additional Locations (2) - [`tests/sentry/notifications/notification_action/test_metric_alert_registry_handlers.py#L264-L270`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/5452b679b365ccf89bb5aa3901679786de2f4243/tests/sentry/notifications/notification_action/test_metric_alert_registry_handlers.py#L264-L270) - [`src/sentry/notifications/platform/templates/metric_alert.py#L19-L21`](https://github.com/getsentry/sentry/blob/5452b679b365ccf89bb5aa3901679786de2f4243/src/sentry/notifications/platform/templates/metric_alert.py#L19-L21)
